title: Fra Lippo Lippi
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am poor brother Lippo, by your leave!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You need not clap your torches to my face.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Zooks, whatן¿½s to blame? you think you see a monk!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What, ן¿½tis past midnight, and you go the rounds,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And here you catch me at an alleyן¿½s end
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where sportive ladies leave their doors ajar?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Carmineן¿½s my cloister: hunt it up,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Do, harry out, if you must show your zeal,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whatever rat, there, haps on his wrong hole,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And nip each softling of a wee white mouse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weke, weke, thatן¿½s crept to keep him company!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Aha, you know your betters! Then, youן¿½ll take
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your hand away thatן¿½s fiddling on my throat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And please to know me likewise. Who am I?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, one, sir, who is lodging with a friend
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Three streets off, heן¿½s a certain . . . how dן¿½ye call?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Master, a . . .Cosimo of the Medici,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ the house that caps the corner. Boh! you were best!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Remember and tell me, the day youן¿½re hanged,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How you affected such a gulletן¿½s-gripe!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But you, sir, it concerns you that your knaves
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pick up a manner nor discredit you:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Zooks, are we pilchards, that they sweep the streets
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And count fair price what comes into their net?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s Judas to a tittle, that man is!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just such a face! Why, sir, you make amends.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lord, Iן¿½m not angry! Bid your hang-dogs go
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Drink out this quarter-florin to the health
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the munificent House that harbours me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And many more beside, lads! more beside!)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And allן¿½s come square again. Iן¿½d like his face
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His, elbowing on his comrade in the door
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the pike and lantern, for the slave that holds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ John Baptistן¿½s head a-dangle by the hair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With one hand (ן¿½Look you, now,ן¿½ as who should say)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his weapon in the other, yet unwiped!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s not your chance to have a bit of chalk,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A wood-coal or the like? or you should see!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yes, Iן¿½m the painter, since you style me so.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What, brother Lippoן¿½s doings, up and down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You know them and they take you? like enough!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I saw the proper twinkle in your eye
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tell you, I liked your looks at very first.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Letן¿½s sit and set things straight now, hip to haunch.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hereן¿½s spring come, and the nights one makes up bands
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To roam the town and sing out carnival,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½ve been three weeks shut within my mew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A-painting for the great man, saints and saints
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And saints again. I could not paint all night
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ouf! I leaned out of window for fresh air.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There came a hurry of feet and little feet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A sweep of lute strings, laughs, and whifts of song,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flower oן¿½ the broom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Take away love, and our earth is a tomb!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flower oן¿½ the quince,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I let Lisa go, and what good in life since?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flower oן¿½ the thyme, and so on. Round they went.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scarce had they turned the corner when a titter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like the skipping of rabbits by moonlight, three slim shapes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a face that looked up . . . zooks, sir, flesh and blood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s all Iן¿½m made of! Into shreds it went,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Curtain and counterpane and coverlet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All the bed-furniture, a dozen knots,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was a ladder! Down I let myself,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hands and feet, scrambling somehow, and so dropped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And after them. I came up with the fun
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hard by Saint Laurence, hail fellow, well met,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flower oן¿½ the rose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If Iן¿½ve been merry, what matter who knows?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so as I was stealing back again
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To get to bed and have a bit of sleep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere I rise up to-morrow and go work
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Jerome knocking at his poor old breast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With his great round stone to subdue the flesh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You snap me of the sudden. Ah, I see!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though your eye twinkles still, you shake your head
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mineן¿½s shaved, a monk, you say, the sting ן¿½s in that!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If Master Cosimo announced himself,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mumן¿½s the word naturally; but a monk!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Come, what am I a beast for? tell us, now!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I was a baby when my mother died
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And father died and left me in the street.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I starved there, God knows how, a year or two
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On fig-skins, melon-parings, rinds and shucks,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Refuse and rubbish. One fine frosty day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My stomach being empty as your hat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wind doubled me up and down I went.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Aunt Lapaccia trussed me with one hand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Its fellow was a stinger as I knew)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so along the wall, over the bridge,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the straight cut to the convent. Six words there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While I stood munching my first bread that month:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So, boy, youן¿½re minded,ן¿½ quoth the good fat father
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wiping his own mouth, ן¿½twas refection-time,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To quit this very miserable world?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will you renounceן¿½ . . . ן¿½the mouthful of bread?ן¿½ thought I;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By no means! Brief, they made a monk of me;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I did renounce the world, its pride and greed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Palace, farm, villa, shop, and banking-house,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Trash, such as these poor devils of Medici
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have given their hearts to, all at eight years old.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, sir, I found in time, you may be sure,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas not for nothing, the good bellyful,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The warm serge and the rope that goes all round,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And day-long blessed idleness beside!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Letן¿½s see what the urchinן¿½s fit forן¿½, that came next.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not overmuch their way, I must confess.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such a to-do! They tried me with their books:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lord, theyן¿½d have taught me Latin in pure waste!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flower oן¿½ the clove.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All the Latin I construe is, ן¿½amoן¿½ I love!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, mind you, when a boy starves in the streets
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eight years together, as my fortune was,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Watching folkן¿½s faces to know who will fling
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The bit of half-stripped grape-bunch he desires,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And who will curse or kick him for his pains,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which gentleman processional and fine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Holding a candle to the Sacrament,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will wink and let him lift a plate and catch
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The droppings of the wax to sell again,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or holla for the Eight and have him whipped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How say I? nay, which dog bites, which lets drop
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His bone from the heap of offal in the street,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, soul and sense of him grow sharp alike,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He learns the look of things, and none the less
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For admonition from the hunger-pinch.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I had a store of such remarks, be sure,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which, after I found leisure, turned to use.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I drew menן¿½s faces on my copy-books,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scrawled them within the antiphonaryן¿½s marge,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Joined legs and arms to the long music-notes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Found eyes and nose and chin for Aן¿½s and Bן¿½s,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And made a string of pictures of the world
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Betwixt the ins and outs of verb and noun,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the wall, the bench, the door. The monks looked black.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Nay,ן¿½ quoth the Prior, ן¿½turn him out, dן¿½ye say?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In no wise. Lose a crow and catch a lark.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What if at last we get our man of parts,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We Carmelites, like those Camaldolese
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Preaching Friars, to do our church up fine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And put the front on it that ought to be!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hereupon he bade me daub away.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thank you! my head being crammed, the walls a blank,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Never was such prompt disemburdening.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ First, every sort of monk, the black and white,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I drew them, fat and lean: then, folk at church,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From good old gossips waiting to confess
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their cribs of barrel-droppings, candle-ends,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the breathless fellow at the altar-foot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fresh from his murder, safe and sitting there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the little children round him in a row
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of admiration, half for his beard and half
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For that white anger of his victimן¿½s son
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shaking a fist at him with one fierce arm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Signing himself with the other because of Christ
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Whose sad face on the cross sees only this
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ After the passion of a thousand years)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till some poor girl, her apron oן¿½er her head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Which the intense eyes looked through) came at eve
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On tiptoe, said a word, dropped in a loaf,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her pair of earrings and a bunch of flowers
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (The brute took growling), prayed, and so was gone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I painted all, then cried ן¿½ן¿½Tis ask and have;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Choose, for moreן¿½s ready!ן¿½, laid the ladder flat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And showed my covered bit of cloister-wall.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The monks closed in a circle and praised loud
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till checked, taught what to see and not to see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Being simple bodies, ן¿½Thatן¿½s the very man!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Look at the boy who stoops to pat the dog!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That womanן¿½s like the Priorן¿½s niece who comes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To care about his asthma: itן¿½s the life!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But there my triumphן¿½s straw-fire flared and funked;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their betters took their turn to see and say:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Prior and the learned pulled a face
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And stopped all that in no time. ן¿½How? whatן¿½s here?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quite from the mark of painting, bless us all!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Faces, arms, legs, and bodies like the true
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As much as pea and pea! itן¿½s devilן¿½s-game!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your business is not to catch men with show,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With homage to the perishable clay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But lift them over it, ignore it all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Make them forget thereן¿½s such a thing as flesh.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your business is to paint the souls of men
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Manן¿½s soul, and itן¿½s a fire, smoke . . . no, itן¿½s not . . .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s vapour done up like a new-born babe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (In that shape when you die it leaves your mouth)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s . . . well, what matters talking, itן¿½s the soul!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give us no more of body than shows soul!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hereן¿½s Giotto, with his Saint a-praising God,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That sets us praising, why not stop with him?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why put all thoughts of praise out of our head
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With wonder at lines, colours, and what not?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Paint the soul, never mind the legs and arms!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rub all out, try at it a second time.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, that white smallish female with the breasts,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sheן¿½s just my niece . . . Herodias, I would say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who went and danced and got menן¿½s heads cut off!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have it all out!ן¿½ Now, is this sense, I ask?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A fine way to paint soul, by painting body
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So ill, the eye canן¿½t stop there, must go further
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And canן¿½t fare worse! Thus, yellow does for white
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When what you put for yellowן¿½s simply black,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And any sort of meaning looks intense
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When all beside itself means and looks nought.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why canן¿½t a painter lift each foot in turn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Left foot and right foot, go a double step,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Make his flesh liker and his soul more like,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Both in their order? Take the prettiest face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Priorן¿½s niece . . . patron-saint, is it so pretty
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You canן¿½t discover if it means hope, fear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sorrow or joy? wonן¿½t beauty go with these?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Suppose Iן¿½ve made her eyes all right and blue,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Canן¿½t I take breath and try to add lifeן¿½s flash,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then add soul and heighten them three-fold?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or say thereן¿½s beauty with no soul at all
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (I never saw it, put the case the same)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If you get simple beauty and nought else,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You get about the best thing God invents:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s somewhat: and youן¿½ll find the soul you have missed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Within yourself, when you return him thanks.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Rub all out!ן¿½ Well, well, thereן¿½s my life, in short,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so the thing has gone on ever since.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½m grown a man no doubt, Iן¿½ve broken bounds:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You should not take a fellow eight years old
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And make him swear to never kiss the girls.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½m my own master, paint now as I please,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Having a friend, you see, in the Corner-house!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lord, itן¿½s fast holding by the rings in front,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Those great rings serve more purposes than just
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To plant a flag in, or tie up a horse!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yet the old schooling sticks, the old grave eyes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are peeping oן¿½er my shoulder as I work,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The heads shake still, ן¿½Itן¿½s artן¿½s decline, my son!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½re not of the true painters, great and old;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brother Angelicoן¿½s the man, youן¿½ll find;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brother Lorenzo stands his single peer:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fag on at flesh, youן¿½ll never make the third!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flower oן¿½ the pine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You keep your mistr . . . manners, and Iן¿½ll stick to mine!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½m not the third, then: bless us, they must know!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Donן¿½t you think theyן¿½re the likeliest to know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They with their Latin? So, I swallow my rage,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Clench my teeth, suck my lips in tight, and paint
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To please them, sometimes do and sometimes donן¿½t;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For, doing most, thereן¿½s pretty sure to come
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A turn, some warm eve finds me at my saints,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A laugh, a cry, the business of the world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Flower oן¿½ the peach
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Death for us all, and his own life for each!)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And my whole soul revolves, the cup runs over,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The world and lifeן¿½s too big to pass for a dream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I do these wild things in sheer despite,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And play the fooleries you catch me at,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In pure rage! The old mill-horse, out at grass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ After hard years, throws up his stiff heels so,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Although the miller does not preach to him
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The only good of grass is to make chaff.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What would men have? Do they like grass or no
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ May they or maynן¿½t they? all I wantן¿½s the thing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Settled for ever one way. As it is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You tell too many lies and hurt yourself:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You donן¿½t like what you only like too much,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You do like what, if given you at your word,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You find abundantly detestable.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For me, I think I speak as I was taught;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I always see the garden and God there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A-making manן¿½s wife: and, my lesson learned,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The value and significance of flesh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I canן¿½t unlearn ten minutes afterwards.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You understand me: Iן¿½m a beast, I know.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But see, now why, I see as certainly
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As that the morning-starן¿½s about to shine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What will hap some day. Weן¿½ve a youngster here
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Comes to our convent, studies what I do,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Slouches and stares and lets no atom drop:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His name is Guidi, heן¿½ll not mind the monks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They call him Hulking Tom, he lets them talk
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He picks my practice up, heן¿½ll paint apace.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I hope so, though I never live so long,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I know whatן¿½s sure to follow. You be judge!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You speak no Latin more than I, belike;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ However, youן¿½re my man, youן¿½ve seen the world
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The beauty and the wonder and the power,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The shapes of things, their colours, lights and shades,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Changes, surprises, and God made it all!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For what? Do you feel thankful, ay or no,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For this fair townן¿½s face, yonder riverן¿½s line,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mountain round it and the sky above,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Much more the figures of man, woman, child,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These are the frame to? Whatן¿½s it all about?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To be passed over, despised? or dwelt upon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wondered at? oh, this last of course! you say.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But why not do as well as say, paint these
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just as they are, careless what comes of it?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Godן¿½s works, paint any one, and count it crime
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To let a truth slip. Donן¿½t object, ן¿½His works
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are here already; nature is complete:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Suppose you reproduce her, (which you canן¿½t)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s no advantage! you must beat her, then.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For, donן¿½t you mark? weן¿½re made so that we love
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ First when we see them painted, things we have passed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perhaps a hundred times nor cared to see;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so they are better, painted, better to us,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which is the same thing. Art was given for that;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ God uses us to help each other so,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lending our minds out. Have you noticed, now,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your cullionן¿½s hanging face? A bit of chalk,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And trust me but you should, though! How much more,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I drew higher things with the same truth!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That were to take the Priorן¿½s pulpit-place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Interpret God to all of you! Oh, oh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It makes me mad to see what men shall do
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we in our graves! This worldן¿½s no blot for us,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor blank; it means intensely, and means good:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To find its meaning is my meat and drink.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ay, but you donן¿½t so instigate to prayer!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Strikes in the Prior: ן¿½when your meaningן¿½s plain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It does not say to folk, remember matins,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or, mind you fast next Friday!ן¿½ Why, for this
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What need of art at all? A skull and bones,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Two bits of stick nailed crosswise, or, whatן¿½s best,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A bell to chime the hour with, does as well.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I painted a Saint Laurence six months since
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At Prato, splashed the fresco in fine style:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How looks my painting, now the scaffoldן¿½s down?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I ask a brother: ן¿½Hugely,ן¿½ he returns
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Already not one phiz of your three slaves
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who turn the Deacon off his toasted side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Butן¿½s scratched and prodded to our heartן¿½s content,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The pious people have so eased their own
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With coming to say prayers there in a rage:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We get on fast to see the bricks beneath.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Expect another job this time next year,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For pity and religion grow iן¿½ the crowd
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your painting serves its purpose!ן¿½ Hang the fools!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That is, youן¿½ll not mistake an idle word
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Spoke in a huff by a poor monk, God wot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tasting the air this spicy night which turns
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The unaccustomed head like Chianti wine!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, the church knows! donן¿½t misreport me, now!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s natural a poor monk out of bounds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Should have his apt word to excuse himself:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hearken how I plot to make amends.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I have bethought me: I shall paint a piece
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ . . . Thereן¿½s for you! Give me six months, then go, see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Something in Santן¿½ Ambrogioן¿½s! Bless the nuns!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They want a cast oן¿½ my office. I shall paint
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ God in the midst, Madonna and her babe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ringed by a bowery, flowery angel-brood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lilies and vestments and white faces, sweet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As puff on puff of grated orris-root
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When ladies crowd to Church at midsummer.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then iן¿½ the front, of course a saint or two,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saint Johnן¿½ because he saves the Florentines,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saint Ambrose, who puts down in black and white
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The conventן¿½s friends and gives them a long day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Job, I must have him there past mistake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The man of Uz (and Us without the z,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Painters who need his patience). Well, all these
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Secured at their devotion, up shall come
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of a corner when you least expect,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As one by a dark stair into a great light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Music and talking, who but Lippo! I!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mazed, motionless, and moonstruck, Iן¿½m the man!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Back I shrink, what is this I see and hear?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I, caught up with my monkן¿½s-things by mistake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My old serge gown and rope that goes all round,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I, in this presence, this pure company!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereן¿½s a hole, whereן¿½s a corner for escape?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then steps a sweet angelic slip of a thing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Forward, puts out a soft palm, ן¿½Not so fast!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Addresses the celestial presence, ן¿½nay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He made you and devised you, after all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though heן¿½s none of you! Could Saint John there draw
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His camel-hair make up a painting brush?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We come to brother Lippo for all that,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iste perfecit opus! So, all smile
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I shuffle sideways with my blushing face
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Under the cover of a hundred wings
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thrown like a spread of kirtles when youן¿½re gay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And play hot cockles, all the doors being shut,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till, wholly unexpected, in there pops
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hothead husband! Thus I scuttle off
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To some safe bench behind, not letting go
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The palm of her, the little lily thing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That spoke the good word for me in the nick,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like the Priorן¿½s niece . . . Saint Lucy, I would say.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so allן¿½s saved for me, and for the church
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A pretty picture gained. Go, six months hence!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your hand, sir, and good-bye: no lights, no lights!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The streetן¿½s hushed, and I know my own way back,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Donן¿½t fear me! Thereן¿½s the grey beginning. Zooks!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
